elements.sent-2411-001: Quark masses are fundamental parameters in QCD, and their accurate knowledge is essential for precision physics.
elements.sent-2411-002: For instance, uncertainties in the heavy-quark $\overline{\rm MS}$ masses contribute to theoretical errors in the corresponding Yukawa couplings to the Higgs field.
elements.sent-2411-003: Their determination is crucial for testing the mechanism of electroweak symmetry breaking.
elements.sent-2411-004: The precision of heavy-quark masses also plays an important role in flavor physics.
elements.sent-2411-005: Heavy-quark ($b, c$) masses have already been determined by many lattice investigations and we refer to Ref.~\cite{FlavourLatticeAveragingGroupFLAG:2024oxs for an overview of the available results.}
elements.sent-2411-006: The main tools to relate the lattice to the customary modified minimal subtraction $\big(\overline{\rm MS}\big)$ results used in these studies are the regularization-independent momentum subtraction (RI-MOM) scheme~\cite{Martinelli:1994ty , its variant regularization-independent symmetric momentum subtraction (RI-SMOM) scheme~\cite{Sturm:2009kb , the quark current (or current-like) correlator method~\cite{HPQCD:2008kxl , or the minimal renormalon-subtracted (MRS) scheme~\cite{Brambilla:2017hcq .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: In these proceedings, we propose an alternative way to determine the heavy-quark mass on the lattice based on the gradient-flow formalism~\cite{Narayanan:2006rf,Luscher:2010iy,Luscher:2013cpa .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Its potential to extract $\alpha_s(M_Z^2)$ from lattice data has recently been explored in Refs.\,\cite{Hasenfratz:2023bok,Wong:2023jvr,Schierholz:2024lge .}
elements.sent-2411-010: The renormalized mass of the quark field $\psi_i$ in the $\MSb$ scheme is denoted by $\overline{m _i$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Even though both numerator and denominator in Eq.~\eqref{eq1 are separately ultraviolet divergent, the ratio is finite~\cite{Makino:2014taa because the divergences only affect the wave function renormalization factor \cite{Luscher:2013cpa .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Thus, the continuum limit in a lattice determination of $\mathring{S (t, \overline{m _1, \mbar_2)$ exists.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: On the other hand, one can calculate $\mathring{S (t, \overline{m _1, \mbar_2)$ in perturbation theory, where the result is expressed as a function of $t$, $\alpha_s$, $\mbar_1$, and $\mbar_2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: By matching the perturbative and the lattice results, one should thus be able to determine the $\MSb$ mass(es).
elements.sent-2411-015: The minimal uncertainty achievable within perturbation theory for an observable is determined by nonperturbative effects.
elements.sent-2411-016: Since $\mathring{S (t, \overline{m _1, \mbar_2)$ of Eq.~\eqref{eq1 is a gauge invariant quantity, the leading non-perturbative contributions are given by the dimension-four condensates such as the gluon condensate $\langle F^{a _ {\mu \nu F^{a _ {\mu \nu \rangle $, because gauge and Lorentz invariance prohibit (parametrically larger) dimension-two condensates.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: This argument does not apply to gauge-dependent methods such as the RI-(S)MOM scheme.
elements.sent-2411-018: Other advantages of our proposal are related to the fact that $\mathring{S (t,\mbar_1, \mbar_2)$ is given by one-point functions, and the fact that the noise in its lattice determination is suppressed by the gradient flow.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The perturbative result for $\mathring{S (t, \mbar, \mbar)$ is known at next-to-next-to-leading order QCD in the limit of a small quark mass $\mbar$: Concerning the numerator in Eq.\,\eqref{eq1 , it vanishes in the strictly massless limit.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: The linear mass term, on the other hand, was calculated at $\mathcal{O (\alpha_s)$ in Ref.~\cite{Makino:2014taa}\footnote{To be more precise, it was presented in the arXiv version v2 of Ref.~\cite{Makino:2014taa .}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: The massless limit of the denominator of $\mathring{S (t, \mbar, \mbar)$ was calculated at $\mathcal{O (\alpha_s)$ in Ref.~\cite{Makino:2014taa and at $\mathcal{O (\alpha^2_s)$ in Refs.~\cite{Harlander:2018zpi,Artz:2019bpr .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, the size of a dimensionless combination $\mbar^2 t$ can span a wide range in the heavy-quark cases.
elements.sent-2411-023: Let us impose $a^2 \ll 8 t \ll L^2$ and $8 t \ll \Lambda_{\rm QCD ^{-2}$ for the range of the flow time $t$, where $a^{-1 =4$~GeV is assumed as a reference and $\Lambda_{\rm QCD \sim 0.3$~GeV.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: The first condition is necessary for reasonable lattice simulations of Eq.~\eqref{eq1 , and the second one is imposed from perturbativity.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Then, $0.1 \ll 8 \mbar_c^2 t \ll 20$ in the charm-quark case and $1.0 \ll 8 \mbar_b^2 t \ll 200$ in the bottom-quark case.
elements.sent-2411-026: Therefore, the known results, which are valid for $\mbar^2 t \ll 1$, are not always sufficient.
elements.sent-2411-027: In this paper, we investigate the mass dependence of the numerator of Eq.~\eqref{eq1 , i.e., the quark condensate $\langle \bar{\chi _f \chi_f \rangle$ with $\mbar_f=\mbar$, at $\mathcal{O (\alpha_s)$ in perturbation theory, as a first step toward the mass determination in this framework.}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: We present the first two terms of the small-$\mbar^2 t$ expansion, and the first three terms of the large-$\mbar^2 t$ expansion.
elements.sent-2411-029: Additionally, we present the full mass dependence, which is valid for a wide range of $\mbar^2 t$, based on a numerical computation.
elements.sent-2411-030: For the foundations of the perturbative approach to the QCD gradient flow we refer to Refs.~\cite{Luscher:2011bx,Artz:2019bpr .}
elements.sent-2411-031: In the computation of $\langle \bar{\chi (t,x) \chi(t,x) \rangle$ at $\mathcal{O (\alpha_s)$, we find eight scalar integrals.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Examples include \begin{equation}\int_0^t ds \int_0^s ds' \int_{p,k} \frac{m p^2}{k^2 (p^2+m^2)} e^{-(2t-s+s') p^2-(s+s') k^2-(s-s') (k-p)^2} ,\non \int_0^t ds \int_{p,k} \frac{m}{k^2 (p^2+m^2)} e^{-(2t-s) p^2-s k^2-s(k-p)^2} , \non \int_0^t ds \int_0^t ds' \int_{p,k} \frac{m (p-k)^2}{k^2((p-k)^2+m^2)} e^{-(2t-s-s')p^2-(s+s') k^2-(s+s') (k-p)^2} ,\end{equation} where $\int_{p,k \equiv \int \frac{\mathrm{d ^d p {(2 \pi)^d \frac{\mathrm{d ^d k {(2 \pi)^d}$ with $d=4-2 \epsilon$ and $m$ is the bare mass.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\int_0^t ds \int_0^s ds' \int_{p,k} \frac{m p^2}{k^2 (p^2+m^2)} e^{-(2t-s+s') p^2-(s+s') k^2-(s-s') (k-p)^2} ,\non \int_0^t ds \int_{p,k} \frac{m}{k^2 (p^2+m^2)} e^{-(2t-s) p^2-s k^2-s(k-p)^2} , \non \int_0^t ds \int_0^t ds' \int_{p,k} \frac{m (p-k)^2}{k^2((p-k)^2+m^2)} e^{-(2t-s-s')p^2-(s+s') k^2-(s+s') (k-p)^2} ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-033: Flow-time integrals appear in addition to the loop-momentum integrals.
elements.sent-2411-034: We evaluate these integrals in an expansion in either $m^2 t$ or $1/(m^2 t)$.
elements.sent-2411-035: The standard technique to expand loop integrals under a certain hierarchy is known as ``expansion by regions'' \cite{Beneke:1997zp .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Its application to the gradient flow formalism has been described in Ref.~\cite{Harlander:2021esn .}
elements.sent-2411-037: It was pointed out though that, in the large-$m^2t$ limit, this method is not straightforwardly applicable, because the flow-time integration extends over hard and soft regions simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-038: In the present study, we pursue a different strategy which does not suffer from this problem.
elements.sent-2411-039: Given an integral $I(m^2,t)$, we perform the Laplace transform \be \tilde{I (v,t) \equiv \int_0^{\infty \mathrm{d (m^2) (m^2)^{-v-1 I(m^2,t) .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The inverse transform is given by \be I(m^2,t)=\frac{1 {2 \pi \mathrm{i \int_{-\mathrm{i \infty ^{\mathrm{i \infty \mathrm{d v \tilde{I (v,t) (m^2)^v .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: We then obtain the small-$(m^2 t)$ expansion by \be I(m^2,t;d)=-\sum_{v_0>0 {\rm Res [\tilde{I (v,t;d) (m^2)^v]|_{v=v_0 , \ee where $v_0$ denotes positive singularities of $\tilde{I (v,t) $.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: For $m^2 t \gg 1$, in turn, we obtain the large-$(m^2 t)$ expansion by \be I(m^2,t;d)=\sum_{v_0<0 {\rm Res [\tilde{I (v,t;d) (m^2)^v]|_{v=v_0 , \ee where $v_0$ denotes negative singularities of $\tilde{I (v,t)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: To obtain these expansions, we need to calculate the singularities of the integrand in Eq.~\eqref{Laplace .}
elements.sent-2411-044: This approach is analogous to the ideas found in Refs.~\cite{Neubert:1994vb,Kitano:2022gzy .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Further details will be explained in a future publication.

elements.sent-2411-056: The running coupling is evaluated for four (five) active quark flavors in the case of the charm (bottom) quark.
elements.sent-2411-057: As input parameters, we adopt $\alpha_s(M_Z^2)=0.1179$, $\mbar_b(\mbar_b)=4.18$~GeV and $\mbar_c(\mbar_c)=1.27$~GeV.
elements.sent-2411-058: The figures indicate the validity of the small- and large-$m^2t$ expansions and provide a convincing verification of our expansion method as well, by the agreement of these approximations with the numerical results.
elements.sent-2411-059: Assuming that the corresponding lattice data are available, one can perform a mass determination at the level of the precision of $\mathcal{O (\alpha_s)$ with this perturbative result.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{minipage}{0.5\hsize} \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{SoverRbottom_lattice2024.eps} \end{center} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.5\hsize} \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{SoverRcharm_lattice2024.eps} \end{center} \end{minipage} \caption{$\mathring{S}(t, \mbar, 0)$ as a function of the flow time $t$ for the bottom-quark case ($\mbar=\mbar_b$) (left) and the charm-quark case ($\mbar=\mbar_c$) (right). The gray line shows the $\mathcal{O}(\alpha_s^0)$-result with full mass dependence, and the black line shows the $\mathcal{O}(\alpha_s)$-result with full mass dependence obtained by our numerical study. The small-$(\mbar^2 t)$ expansion and the large-$(\mbar^2 t)$ expansion to $\mathcal{O}(\alpha_s)$ are also shown. (The large-$(\mbar^2 t)$ expansion is not relevant to the charm quark case.)} \label{fig}
elements.sent-2411-060: The gradient-flow formalism has proven useful already in many different contexts.
elements.sent-2411-061: In this contribution, we proposed a new method to determine heavy-quark masses by utilizing quark bilinear operators defined in the gradient-flow formalism.
elements.sent-2411-062: Eq.~\eqref{eq1 is manifestly gauge invariant and simply a combination of one-point functions defined in the gradient flow.}
elements.sent-2411-063: These features have the potential to contribute to a precise determination of the quark masses.
elements.sent-2411-064: To perform the mass determination in this framework, one needs a perturbative result to match with lattice data.
elements.sent-2411-065: We calculated the quark condensate $\langle \bar{\chi (t,x) \chi(t,x) \rangle$ at $\mathcal{O (\alpha_s)$, keeping its quark mass dependence in an expansion in either $\mbar^2t$ or $1/(\mbar^2t)$, and by a numerical computation valid for a wide range of $\mbar^2t$.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: While the first few terms of the small- and large-$\mbar^2 t$ expansions were given here, we will extend these expansions further and present them in a future paper.
elements.sent-2411-067: We will also study the mass dependence of $\langle \bar{\chi (t,x) \overleftrightarrow{\slashed{D \chi(t,x) \rangle$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Furthermore, one may study other observables such as $1-\langle \bar{\chi _1(t,x) \overleftrightarrow{\slashed{D \chi_1(t,x) \rangle/\langle \bar{\chi _2(t,x) \overleftrightarrow{\slashed{D \chi_2(t,x) \rangle$, where $\chi_1$ is a heavy and $\chi_2$ is a massless quark.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: This observable exhibits the heavy-quark mass dependence of $\mathcal{O (\mbar^2)$ for small $\mbar^2 t$ and is therefore expected to be highly sensitive to the quark mass, while, for instance, $\mathring{S (t, \mbar, 0)$ and $\mathring{S (t, \mbar, \mbar)$ exhibit the $\mathcal{O (\mbar^1)$ dependence.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Although the determination of heavy-quark masses motivated us to study the mass dependence for a wide range of $\mbar^2 t$, this framework should be applicable to the determination of light-quark masses as well.
elements.sent-2411-071: For a precise determination, it is crucial to calculate higher orders in $\alpha_s$.
elements.sent-2411-072: A perturbative calculation at $\mathcal{O (\alpha_s^2)$ with mass dependence might be feasible.}
elements.sent-2411-073: It is also theoretically important to address the structure of the small-flow-time expansion for these observables, as understanding this structure can help reduce or properly account for theoretical uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-074: Lastly, the precision that can be achieved on the lattice in practice is crucial and remains to be investigated.
